موٹا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀫𑁄𑀝𑁆𑀝 (*moṭṭa), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀫𑁄𑀝 (*moṭa). Cognate with Punjabi موٹا (moṭā).

مؤثر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُؤَثِّر (mu'assir), from Arabic مُؤَثِّر (muʔaṯṯir).

senses.g: current (in relation to time – present; in the moment)

موجود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَوْجُود (mawjūd), itself from Arabic مَوْجُود (mawjūd), from the Arabic root و ج د (w j d).

موجودگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موجودگی (mawjūdagī). Equivalent of مَوجُودَہ (maujūda, “present, attendant”) + ـگی (-gī, “-ance, -y”).

موجودہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موجوده (mawjūda), from Arabic مَوْجُودَة (mawjūda), feminine of مَوْجُود (mawjūd).

senses.g: muezzin (the person who issues the call to prayer from one of the minarets of a mosque)

مؤذن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُؤَذِّن (mu'azzin), from Arabic مُؤَذِّن (muʔaḏḏin).

مور.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑁄𑀭 (mora), from Sanskrit मयूर (mayū́ra).

مورت.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit मूर्ति (mūrti, “embodiment, form”).

مورتی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit मूर्ति (mūrti). Doublet of مورت (mūrat, semilearned borrowing).

موز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَوْز (mawz), from Arabic مَوْز (mawz).

senses.g: Mozambique (a country in East Africa and Southern Africa)

مؤسسہ.e: Borrowed from Persian مؤسسه (mo'assese), from Arabic مُؤَسَّسَة (muʔassasa).

موسم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَوْسِم (mawsim), from Arabic مَوْسِم (mawsim).

senses.g: related to; in relation to (a subject)

موسوم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موسوم (mawsūm), from Arabic مَوْسُوم (mawsūm).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

موسی.e: Borrowed from Arabic مُوسَى (mūsā), from Biblical Hebrew.

senses.g: Musakhail (a city in Musakhail district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
panpipes an instrument/pipe used by shepherd's or devises
an instrument (ie. to describe a human as an instrument – not necessarily dehumanizing)

موسیقار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موسیقار (mūsīqār), from Arabic مُوسِيقَار (mūsīqār, “musician”), from Classical Syriac ܡܘܣܝܩܪܐ (mūsīqārā, “musician”), from Ancient Greek μουσικός (mousikós, “musician”).

موسیقی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُوسِیقِی (mūsīqī), from Arabic مُوسِيقَى (mūsīqā), ultimately from Ancient Greek μουσική (mousikḗ). First attested in 1792 as Middle Hindi موسیقی (mvsyqy).

موضع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موضع (mawzi', mawza'), from Arabic مَوْضِع (mawḍiʕ) / مَوْضَع (mawḍaʕ).

موضوع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موضوع (mawzū'), from Arabic مَوْضُوع (mawḍūʕ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian موضوع (mawzū'), from Arabic مَوْضُوع (mawḍūʕ).

موضوعات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موضوعات (mawzū'āt), from Arabic مَوْضُوع (mawḍūʕ).

موقع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موقع (mauqi', mauqa', “time”), from Arabic مَوْقِع (mawqiʕ).

مول.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀽𑀮 (mūla), from Sanskrit मूल (mūla). Cognate with Bengali মূল (mul), Gujarati મૂળ (mūḷ).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀽𑀮 (mūla), from Sanskrit मूल (mūla). Cognate with Bengali মূল (mul), Gujarati મૂળ (mūḷ).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑁄𑀮𑁆𑀮 (mŏlla), from Sanskrit मौल्य (maulya).

مولا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَوْلَا (mawlā), from Arabic مَوْلًى (mawlan).

senses.g: birthday, nativity, especially that of Muhammad

موم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian موم (mōm, mūm). Compare Bengali মোম (mōm), Punjabi موم (mom).

مومن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُؤْمِن (mu'min), borrowed from Arabic مُؤْمِن (muʔmin), from آمَنَ (ʔāmana). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi مومن (mvmn /⁠momin⁠/).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

مومن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُؤْمِن (mu'min), borrowed from Arabic مُؤْمِن (muʔmin), from آمَنَ (ʔāmana). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi مومن (mvmn /⁠momin⁠/).

senses.g: a female given name from Arabic, masculine equivalent مومِن (momin)
Montenegro (a country on the Balkan Peninsula in Southeast Europe)

مؤنث.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُؤَنَّث (mu'annas), from Arabic مُؤَنَّث (muʔannaṯ).

senses.g: a muhajir: Urdu-speaking Muslim that moved to Pakistan after the Partition of India

مہاجر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُهَاجِر (muhājir), from Arabic مُهَاجِر (muhājir, “migrant”), from هَاجَرَ (hājara).

مہاجرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُهاجَرَت (muhājarat), from Arabic مُهَاجَرَة (muhājara).

senses.g: skill, expertness, proficiency, excellency (in a profession)

مہارت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَهَارَت (mahārat), borrowed from Arabic مَهَارَة (mahāra), from مَهَرَ (mahara).

مہایان.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit महायान (mahāyāna, literally “great vehicle”); equivalent to مہا (mahā, “great”) + یان (yān, “vehicle”).

senses.g: a male given name from Chagatai

مہدی قلی.e: From Chagatai مهدیقلی (mhdy-qly, literally “slave of the Mahdi”); compare Azerbaijani Mehdiqulu, Turkish Mehdikulu.

مہر.e: From Arabic verbal noun مَهْر (mahr), from verb مَهَرَ (mahara, “to contract, to give a dowry”).

Borrowed from Classical Persian مهر (mihr), from Middle Persian mtr' (Mihr), from Old Persian 𐎷𐎰𐎼 (Miθra), from Avestan 𐬨𐬌𐬚𐬭𐬀 (miθra, “covenant, contract, oath”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *mitra- (“covenant, treaty, agreement, promise”) Compare Sanskrit मित्र (mitra).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مهر (muhr), from Middle Persian [Book Pahlavi needed] (mwdl), 𐫖𐫇𐫍𐫡 (mwhr /⁠muhr⁠/, “seal”).

senses.g: sun and moon (in contrast to special people)
the Indus Valley; the Indus country

مہران.e: From Classical Persian مهران (mihrān). Cognate with Punjabi مہران (mehrān) and Sindhi مَهَراڻُ (maharāṇu), Persian borrowings. An ancient Persianate name for the Indus, it was already in use during the earliest medieval era. The accomplished Arab grammarian Ibn Duraid in the 9ᵗʰ century used the name to describe the "great river" in what is today Pakistan.

senses.g: loving, affectionate, friendly, kind, benevolent, beneficent, favouring, indulgent, gracious, propitious; compassionate, merciful

مہرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُهْرَه (muhra, “bead, vertebra, game piece”).

مہکار.e: From مَہَک (mahak) or کار (kār). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi مہکار (mhkar).

senses.g: vocative plural of مَہَک (mahak, “fragrance; scent; aroma”)
oblique plural of مَہَک (mahak, “fragrance; scent; aroma”)
direct plural of مَہَک (mahak, “fragrance; scent; aroma”)
respite, delay; extension; grace period (extra time before a deadline or before something bad incurs)

مہلت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مُهْلَت (muhlat), borrowed from Arabic مُهْلَة (muhla), from مَهَلَ (mahala). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi مہلت (mhlt /⁠muhlat⁠/).

مہمان دار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مهماندار (mihmān-dār). Equivalent to مہمان (mehmān) + ـدار (-dār).
Borrowed from Classical Persian مهماندار (mihmān-dār). Equivalent to مہمان (mehmān) + ـدار (-dār).

مہمان داری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مهمانداری (mihmān-dārī). Equivalent to مہمان دار (mehmān-dār, “host, hospitable”) + ـی (-ī).

مہندس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مهندس (muhandis), from Arabic مُهَنْدِس (muhandis), from هَنْدَسَة (handasa, “engineering”), itself borrowed from Middle Persian.

مہندسی.e: From Classical Persian مهندسی (muhandisī), from مهندس (muhandis), from Arabic مُهَنْدِس (muhandis), from هَنْدَسَة (handasa, “engineering”), from Middle Persian.

senses.g: Mendhar (a town, a tehsil in Poonch district, Jammu and Kashmir, India)

مہنگا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀫𑀳𑀕𑁆𑀖 (mahaggha), from Sanskrit महार्घ (mahārgha).

senses.g: a month; period of thirty days

مہینا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مهینا (mahīnā). Doublet of مَاہ (māh)

senses.g: Mianwali (a city in Mianwali district, province of Punjab, Pakistan)

میانوالی.e: Named after Akbar the Great's son, Mian Ali + Urdu والی (vālī).

ex.u: کْیا بات کَر رَہے ہو مِیاں؟
ex.r: kyā bāt kar rahe ho miyā̃
ex.e: What are you talking about, mister?

میاں.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian میان (miyān, “center, (India) lord, sir”). First attested as Old Hindi मींयां (mī̃yā̃).

میٹھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀺𑀝𑁆𑀞 (miṭṭha), from Sanskrit मिष्ट (miṣṭa).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀺𑀝𑁆𑀞 (miṭṭha), from Sanskrit मिष्ट (miṣṭa).

میٹھی عید.e: From میٹھی (mīṭhī, “sweet”) + عید (īd, “Eid”), literally “sweet Eid, Sweet Festival”.

میخانہ.e: First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi میخانہ (myxanh), borrowed from Classical Persian مَیخَانَه (mayxāna).

میدان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مَیْدَان (maydān), borrowed from Arabic مَيْدَان (maydān).

senses.g: a female given name, Medha, from Sanskrit

میرا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi मेरौ (merau), मेरा (merā), from Apabhramsa महारिय (mahāriya), मेर (mera) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-). See there for more. Cognate with Assamese মোৰ (mür), Gujarati મારું (mārũ), Punjabi ਮੇਰਾ (merā), Romani miro and Classical Persian مرا (marā).

Inherited from Old Hindi मेरौ (merau), मेरा (merā), from Apabhramsa महारिय (mahāriya), मेर (mera) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-). See there for more. Cognate with Assamese মোৰ (mür), Gujarati મારું (mārũ), Punjabi ਮੇਰਾ (merā), Romani miro and Classical Persian مرا (marā).

میراث.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian میراث (mīrās), from Arabic مِيرَاث (mīrāṯ).

senses.g: Mirpur (the second largest city in Azad Kashmir, Pakistan, located in the Mirpur district)
Mirpur (a union council in Abbottabad district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)
Mirpur Khas (a city in Mirpur Khas district, Sindh, Pakistan)

میرپور خاص.e: From میر (mer, “Mir (title) of Khairpur (princely state)”, the ruler of the Talpur dynasty) + پور (por) + خاص (xās).

senses.g: Mirpur Mathelo (a city in Ghotki district, Sindh, Pakistan)

میز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian میز (mēz), possibly influenced by Portuguese mesa.

میزان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian میزان (mīzān), from Arabic مِيزَان (mīzān).

میش.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit मेष (meṣá). Influenced by Classical Persian میش (mēš).
Borrowed from Sanskrit मेष (meṣá). Influenced by Classical Persian میش (mēš).

senses.g: cloud; especially a dark cloud, storm cloud

میغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian میغ (mēġ, “cloud; fog, mist”). Doublet of مین٘ہ (mẽh).

میکائیل.e: Learned borrowing from Arabic مِيكَائِيل (mīkāʔīl), ultimately from Biblical Hebrew מִיכָאֵל (miká'el).

میل.e: Borrowed from Arabic مِيل (mīl), from Latin mīlle passuum.
Inherited from Sanskrit मल॑ (mála). Compare Punjabi مَیࣇ (maiḷ) / ਮੈਲ਼ (maiḷ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian میل (mayl), from Arabic مَيْل (mayl).

میلاد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مِیلَاد (mīlād), borrowed from Arabic مِيلَاد (mīlād).

senses.g: a surname, Memon, from Gujarati or Sindhi

میمن.e: Borrowed from Gujarati મેમણ (měmaṇ) or Sindhi مَيمَڻ /मैमण (maimaṇ). Doublet of مومِن (momin)

مینڈک.e: Inherited from Prakrit मंडुक्क (maṃḍukka), from Sanskrit म॒ण्डूक॑ (maṇḍū́ka).

مینڈھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑁂𑀁𑀠 (meṃḍha), from Sanskrit मेण्ढ (meṇḍha), from Munda.

senses.g: Mingora (a city in Swat district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)

مینو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian مینو (mīnō, mīnū, “emerald; paradise”).

مینہ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑁂𑀳 (meha), from Sanskrit मेघ (megha, “cloud”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hmaygʰás (“cloud”). Doublet of میغ (meġ).

senses.g: first-person singular subjective personal pronoun: I

میں.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Apabhramsa मइं (maĩ), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀏 (mae), from Sanskrit मया (mayā).

ex.u: چُھٹِّیوں میں مَیں کَشْمِیر جا رَہا ہُوں۔
ex.r: chuṭṭiyõ mẽ ma͠i kaśmīr jā rahā hū̃.
ex.e: I'm going to Kashmir during the holidays.
within the budget of fifty rupees

ex.u: لوگوں میں وہ پَیسے تَقْسِیم کَر دی۔
ex.r: logõ mẽ vo paise taqsīm kar dī.
ex.e: He divided the money among the people.

میں.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀫𑀚𑁆𑀛𑁂 (majjhe), from Sanskrit मध्ये (mádhye), locative of मध्य (mádhya, “middle”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *mádʰyas, from Proto-Indo-European *médʰyos. Cognate with Sindhi ۾ (mẽ).

میور.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit मयूर (mayūra). Doublet of مور (mor).

senses.g: Munich (the capital and largest city of Bavaria, Germany)

میونخ.e: Borrowed from English Munich, influenced by German München.

میوہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian میوه (mēwa, “fruit”), from Middle Persian 𐭬𐭩𐭥𐭪 (mēwag).

مئی.e: Borrowed from English May, ultimately from Latin Maius.

مئی مکھی.e: Calque of English mayfly, as مَئی (maī, “May”, Gregorian calendar month) + مَکّھی (makkhī, “fly”).

مے.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian می (may, “wine”), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *médʰu (“honey, mead”). Doublet of مدھو (madhu).

senses.g: corpose, dead body; the deceased (particularly in a funeral)
The thirty-second letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by م and followed by ں.
medial form of ں (ṉ) — Marks the medial nasalization in Nastaliq, with the character ن (nūn) marked with the maghnūna diacritic: respectively ں (̃) - the final form.

نا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀸 (ṇā), from Sanskrit न (na). Doublet of نہ (na).

ex.u: ہاں یا نا میں جواب دو۔
ex.r: hā̃ yā nā mẽ javāb do.

نا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀸 (ṇā), from Sanskrit न (na). Doublet of نہ (na).

ex.u: محبت میں نا آہنگی سے بدتر کوئی اور چیز نہیں ہو سکتی۔
ex.r: maḥabbat meṉ nā āhaṉgī se badtar ko'ī aur cīz nahīṉ ho saktī.
ex.e: There can be nothing worse than dissonance in love.

نا پسند.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناپَسَند. By surface analysis, نا (nā) + پسند.
Borrowed from Classical Persian ناپَسَند. By surface analysis, نا (nā) + پسند.

نا لائق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَالَایِق (nālāyiq). By surface analysis, ناـ (nā-, “un-”) + لائِق (lāiq, “worthy”).

senses.g: one who is incompetent or undignified

نا لائق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَالَایِق (nālāyiq). By surface analysis, ناـ (nā-, “un-”) + لائِق (lāiq, “worthy”).

ناانصافی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَااِنْصَافِی (nāinsāfī). By surface analysis, نا- (nā-) + انصاف + ـی (-ī).

نااہل قرار دینا.e: From ناْاَہَل (nā-ahal, “ineligible”) + قَرار (qarār, “declare”) + دینا (denā, “to (give)”), literally “to declare (one) ineligibile”.

نابھی.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit नाभि (nābhi). Doublet of ناف (nāf).

ناپاک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناپاک (nāpāk). Equivalent to ناـ (nā-, “not, un-, in-”) + پاک (pāk, “clean, pure”).

senses.g: female equivalent of ناتی (nātī): daughter's daughter; granddaughter

ناتی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi ناتی (naty /⁠nātī⁠/), from Old Hindi नांती (nāṃtī /⁠nā̃tī⁠/), from Apabhramsa णत्तिअ (ṇattia), from Prakrit 𑀡𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺𑀅 (ṇattia), from Sanskrit नप्तृ (naptṛ) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-).

ناچ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀘𑁆𑀘 (ṇacca), from Sanskrit नृत्य (nṛtya), which was originally नृत्त (nṛttá) (also see नृत्यति (nṛtyati, “to dance”)). Compare Hindi नृत्य (nŕtya). Cognate with Punjabi ਨੱਚ (nacca) / نَچّ (nacc), Gujarati નાચ (nāc), Sindhi नचु (nacu) / نَچُ (nacu), Bengali নাচ (nac), Chakma 𑄚𑄌𑄴 (nāc), Odia ନାଚ (nāca), Assamese নাচ (nas).

senses.g: a bad workman always blames his tools
to be instrumental (ie. to follow or act on someone's order)

ناچنا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi [Term?], from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀤𑀺 (ṇaccadi), from Sanskrit नृत्यति (nṛ́tyati).

ناخدا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناخدا (nāxudā), from earlier ناوخدا (nāwxudā, literally “ship-master”).

senses.g: nail, fingernail (of the finger or toe), claw, talon

نادر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نادِر, from Arabic نَادِر (nādir).

ناراض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناراض (nā-rāz), from ناـ (nā-) + Arabic رَاضٍ (rāḍin). By surface analysis, نا (nā, “not, un”) + راضی (rāzī, “content”).

نارنجی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نارنجی (nāranjī). Doublet of نارنگی (nāraṅgī).

نارنگی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀡𑀸𑀭𑀁𑀕 (ṇāraṃga), from Sanskrit नारङ्ग (nāráṅga, “the orange tree”) + -इका (-ikā), either ultimately derived from Dravidian, or even possibly further derived from Proto-Austroasiatic *ŋaːm (“sweet”). Doublet of نارنجی (nārañjī), (derived from Classical Persian نارنجی (nāranjī, “bitter orange”), from the very same origin).

Inherited from Prakrit 𑀡𑀸𑀭𑀁𑀕 (ṇāraṃga), from Sanskrit नारङ्ग (nāráṅga, “the orange tree”) + -इका (-ikā), either ultimately derived from Dravidian, or even possibly further derived from Proto-Austroasiatic *ŋaːm (“sweet”). Doublet of نارنجی (nārañjī), (derived from Classical Persian نارنجی (nāranjī, “bitter orange”), from the very same origin).

senses.g: Narowal (a city in Narowal district, Punjab, Pakistan)
Norway (a country in Scandinavia in Northern Europe)

ناریل.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi ناریل (naryl) (c. 1611), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀸𑀭𑀺𑀏𑀮 (ṇāriela), from Sanskrit नारिकेल (nārikela), of Dravidian origin. Sense 3 is a semantic loan from Classical Persian نَارْگِیلَه (nārgīla). Cognate with Gujarati નારિયળ (nāriyaḷ), Marathi नारळ (nāraḷ), Punjabi ਨਾਰੀਅਲ (nārīala), and Sindhi ناريلُ (nārelu).

ناز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناز (nāz), from Parthian nʾz (nāz, “pleasure, delight”).

ex.e: She is a very delicate girl.
I am in a really fragile condition.

نازل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَازِل (nāzil), from Arabic نَازِل (nāzil).

نازیبا.e: From نا (nā, “not; imm; un”) + زیبا (zebā, “befitting, graceful”).

ناسور.e: Borrowed from Arabic نَاسُور (nāsūr), from Classical Syriac ܢܳܨܘܿܪܴܐ (nāṣōrā, “sore, ulcer”).

ناشپاتی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناشپاتی (nāšpātī). Compare Hindi नाशपाती (nāśpātī), Punjabi ਨਾਸ਼ਪਾਤੀ (nāśpātī), Bengali নাশপাতি (naśpati), Uzbek nashvati, Uyghur نەشپۈت (neshpüt).

ناشتا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَاشْتَا (nāštā). First attested in c. 1591 as Middle Hindi ناشتا (naśta).

ناصرت.e: Ultimately from Arabic النَّاصِرَة (an-nāṣira) (where Hebrew נָצְרַת (náṣərat)), possibly influenced by Persian [Term?].

senses.g: Nazareth (a city of Palestine in northern Israel)

ناصور.e: Borrowed from Arabic نَاصُور (nāṣūr), from Classical Syriac ܢܳܨܘܿܪܴܐ (nāṣōrā, “sore, ulcer”).

ناظم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناظم (nāzim), from the active participle of Arabic نَظَمَ (naẓama, “to organize; to compose”).

ناف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناف (nāf). Doublet of نابھی (nābhi). Related to English navel.

نافرمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نافرمان (nāfarmān). By surface analysis, نَاـ (nā-) + فَرْمَان (farmān).

نافرمانبرداری.e: From نا (nā, “not”) + فرماں (farmāṉ, “order; command”) + بردار (bardār, “bearer”) + ی (ī).

senses.g: conch-shell as an instrument, particularly in Hinduism

ex.u:
ویراں ہے سینہ بسکہ ترے باج اے صنم
فریاد دل ہے نغمۂ ناقوس کی نمط
vīrā̃ hai sīnā bas-ki tire bāj ai sanam
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

ناقوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناقوس (nāqūs), from Arabic نَاقُوس (nāqūs), from Classical Syriac ܢܩܘܫܐ (nāqūšā).

ناک.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀓𑁆𑀓 (ṇakka), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *náHs, from Proto-Indo-European *néh₂s. Cognates Cognates include Assamese নাক (nak), Bengali নাক (nak), Odia ନାକ (nāka), Nepali नाक (nāk), Punjabi ਨੱਕ (nakka) / نَکّ (nakk), Saraiki نَکّ (nakk) Sindhi نَڪُ (naku), Marathi नाक (nāk), Gujarati નાક (nāk), and Romani nakh. More distantly related to English nose, French naz, Russian ноздря́ (nozdrjá) and нос (nos), Albanian noçë, and Lithuanian nósis.

senses.g: the entrance to a road or a pass
the point of meeting between two or more roads
an opening/gap (ie. between a hedge; fence; bank etc.)

ex.u: ہر ناگرک کو ایک دوسرے کی مدد کرنا چاہئے
ex.e: Every citizen should help their fellow countrymen.

senses.g: Nall (a city in Khuzdar district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

نال.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit, from Sanskrit नाडी (nāḍī), ultimately from a Dravidian language such as Tamil நாணல் (nāṇal), Kannada ನಳ್ಳು (naḷḷu, “reed”).

نام.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀸𑀫 (ṇāma), from Sanskrit नामन् (nā́man), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *Hnā́ma, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hnā́ma (compare Persian نام (nâm)), from Proto-Indo-European *h₁nómn̥ (“name”). Cognate with Punjabi ناں (nāṉ) and English name.

نامرد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَامَرْد (nāmard). By surface analysis, ناـ (nā-, “not”) + مَرْد (mard, “man, masculine”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian نَامَرْد (nāmard). By surface analysis, ناـ (nā-, “not”) + مَرْد (mard, “man, masculine”).

نامردی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَامَرْدِی (nāmardī). By surface analysis, نامَرْد (nāmard, “impotent”) + ـی (-ī).

ناممکن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ناممکن (nāmumkin). Equivalent to ناـ (nā-, “not, in-”) + ممکن (mumkin, “possible”).

نامنظور.e: Persianate compound of ناـ (nā-, negating prefix) + مَنظُور (manzūr, “accepted”).

نامنظوری.e: Persianate compound of ناـ (nā-, negating prefix) + مَنظُور (manzūr, “accepted”) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: correspondent (journalist who sends reports from abroad)
naan, A type of round, flat bread baked in a tandoor popular in South and Central Asian cuisine.

نان خطائی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَانِ خَطَائِی (nān-i xatā'ī, “Cathayan bread, bread of Cathay”).

senses.g: Imam Hussain and (by extension) his grandfather Muhammad.

نانا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi نانا (nana) (c. 1564), from Prakrit 𑀡𑀡𑁆𑀡 (ṇaṇṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), ultimately an onomatopoeic nursery reduplication

نانگل.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀗𑁆𑀕𑀭 (ṇaṅgara), from Sanskrit लाङ्गल (lāṅgala) or directly borrowed from Dravidian. Compare with Marathi नांगर (nāṅgar), Assamese নাঙল (naṅol), Ollari నాంగల్ (nāṅgal), Malayalam ഞേങ്ങോൽ (ñēṅṅōl).

نانی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀡𑀡𑁆𑀡 (ṇaṇṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-).

ناو.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀸𑀯𑀸 (ṇāvā), from Sanskrit नौ (náu), नावा (nāvā), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *náHuṣ, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *náHuš, from Proto-Indo-European *néh₂us. Cognate with Hindi नाव (nāv), Pali nāvā, and Punjabi ਨਾਉ (nāu). Cognate through Indo-European with Ancient Greek ναῦς (naûs), Persian ناو (nâv), and Spanish nave.

ex.u: ہمارے منشی صاحبِ مہربان کو ابتدائے سن سے قصّہ کہانیوں کا بڑا شوق تھا الف لیلہ ، امیر حمزہ کی داستان کے علاوہ بوستانِ خیال کی کل جلدیں نظر سے گذری ہوئی تھیں کوئی ناول ایسا نہ تھا جو آپ نے نہ دیکھا ہو
ex.e: Our dear clerk has had a great passion for stories since the beginning of time. Apart from One Thousand and One Nights and the Hamzanama, he had read all the volumes of Bostan-e-Khayal. There was no novel that he had not seen.

ناہر.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *नखदर (*nakhadara, literally “tearing with claws”).

نائب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَائِب (nā'ib), from Arabic نَائِب (nāʔib).

نائی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀡𑀸𑀯𑀺𑀤 (ṇāvida), from Sanskrit नापित (nāpitá).

senses.g: Nigeria (a country in the West Africa, Africa)

نبض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نَبْض (nabz), from Arabic نَبْض (nabḍ). First attested in c. 1739 as Middle Hindi نبض (nbẓ /⁠nabz⁠/).
